title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered at a local museum. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Carla, The Audio Guide Voice
Carla volunteered to record audio descriptions for a small industrial museum after a neighbour heard her reading to children at the library during a rainy holiday workshop. [47]She was nervous because the script had to be clear without sounding like a school lesson.[/47] Her first recording was rejected for being too fast, and she learned to leave tiny pauses before technical terms, especially names of engines and weaving parts. Carla works as a nurse, and [48]her experience explaining procedures to anxious patients helped her make complicated machines sound less intimidating.[/48] She spent extra time describing textures for visitors who could not see the exhibits clearly. The most moving response came from a retired engineer who said her description of a loom reminded him of his father's workshop. Carla says she had assumed museums were about objects, but the work showed her they are equally about memory and access for people arriving with very different needs.
texts.B:
### Daniel, The Object Handler
Daniel regularly helped visitors handle replica objects during weekend public sessions in the busy education room. [49]He had to learn to say no firmly when children tried to tap the glass cases with small metal tools.[/49] At first, he worried that strictness would spoil the fun, but families seemed calmer once the rules were explained. Daniel's background as a sports coach helped him give short instructions to energetic groups without raising his voice or making the room feel like school. [50]The object he enjoyed presenting most was a heavy miner's lamp, because visitors always underestimated its weight before holding it.[/50] He also learned to ask older visitors what they personally remembered, rather than assuming the museum had all the correct answers. Daniel says the sessions worked best when people stopped treating history as distant, polished and began comparing it with jobs, streets and habits they already knew from everyday life.
texts.C:
### Mei, The Archive Sorter
Mei joined the small archive team to practise English, but she soon became absorbed in boxes of handwritten letters from factory workers who had lived in nearby streets. [51]Her task was to remove rusty paperclips before they stained fragile original documents any further.[/51] The job sounded dull, yet every envelope carried names, wages, addresses or complaints that made the past feel crowded and alive. Mei found dates surprisingly difficult because some writers used old local workplace abbreviations, so she built a reference sheet for other volunteers. [52]She was delighted when a family historian used one of her carefully labelled letters to identify a great-grandmother's workplace.[/52] The archive room was cold even in summer, and she wore fingerless gloves to keep working for longer morning shifts. Mei now says the role taught her that preservation is mostly quiet sustained patience, not dramatic discoveries, although those sometimes arrive when nobody expects them.
texts.D:
### Patrick, The Front-Hall Greeter
Patrick usually stood near the entrance, welcoming visitors and suggesting routes through the museum. [53]He changed his approach after noticing that people who claimed to have only ten minutes often stayed longer if he recommended one specific exhibit.[/53] Instead of handing out the full map immediately, he asked whether they liked machines, family stories or old photographs. His previous work as a taxi driver helped because he knew which local streets had changed names, and he could connect exhibits to places visitors recognised. [54]The hardest moment came when a school group arrived during a power cut and he had to rearrange their visit around rooms with natural light.[/54] Patrick also kept spare pencils for children completing activity sheets while parents bought tickets. [55]He now trains new greeters to begin with a question rather than a speech.[/55] [56]He judges success by visitors returning to the desk later to tell him what surprised them.[/56]

questions.question: was concerned about making recorded information informative but not patronising?
questions.explanation: Carla worried the audio script had to be clear without sounding like a school lesson.

questions.question: used experience explaining difficult matters to worried people in another profession?
questions.explanation: Carla's nursing experience helped her explain complicated machines less intimidatingly.

questions.question: had to stop young visitors from misusing items near displays?
questions.explanation: Daniel learned to say no when children tried to tap glass cases with metal tools.

questions.question: liked showing an object because people misjudged its physical heaviness?
questions.explanation: Daniel enjoyed presenting the miner's lamp because visitors underestimated its weight.

questions.question: was responsible for preventing a small metal item from damaging documents?
questions.explanation: Mei removed rusty paperclips before they stained fragile documents.

questions.question: was pleased that their labelling helped someone trace a relative's history?
questions.explanation: A family historian used Mei's labelled letter to identify a great-grandmother's workplace.

questions.question: changed their advice after noticing how brief visitors could be encouraged to stay?
questions.explanation: Patrick found that visitors with only ten minutes stayed longer after one specific recommendation.

questions.question: had to alter a planned visit because of an unexpected building problem?
questions.explanation: Patrick rearranged a school group's visit during a power cut.

questions.question: teaches newcomers to start by asking visitors about their interests?
questions.explanation: Patrick trains greeters to begin with a question rather than a speech.

questions.question: measures success by visitors coming back to share a reaction?
questions.explanation: Patrick judges success by visitors returning to say what surprised them.
